greeting: Hey! I'm Kairu. Pick a mode and let's work through some Maths P1. Take your time on the algebra ones — show working in your head before you click.

questions.stem: (-3)² + (-2)² is equal to
questions.explanation: A negative number squared becomes positive: (-3)² = 9 and (-2)² = 4. Adding gives 9 + 4 = 13.

distractors.A: Comes from treating both squares as negative (-9 + -4).
distractors.B: Comes from doing -9 - 1 or other sign mistakes.
distractors.C: Comes from 9 - 4, subtracting instead of adding.

questions.stem: What is the value of the digit 6 in the number 48.621?
questions.explanation: In 48.621 the digit 6 sits in the tenths place — the first position after the decimal point. Its value is therefore 6/10 (or 0.6).

distractors.A: 6/100 would be the hundredths place — that's the position of the 2.
distractors.C: 6 (whole number) would require the digit to be in the units column.
distractors.D: 600 would put it in the hundreds column.

questions.stem: A certain amount of money is shared among Jay, Troy and Jordan in the ratio 2:3:9. If Troy received $40 more than Jay, then the amount of money shared is
questions.explanation: Troy gets 3 parts and Jay gets 2 parts, so the difference is 1 part = $40. The total is 2 + 3 + 9 = 14 parts, so 14 × $40 = $560.

distractors.A: $280 = 7 × $40 — that's only half the total parts.
distractors.B: $360 = 9 × $40 — that's just Jordan's share, not the total.

questions.explanation: The union collects every element appearing in any of the sets — list each one once. Combining gives {2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}.

distractors.A: {2} would be the intersection P ∩ Q ∩ S, not the union.
distractors.B: {2, 3} appears in P and Q only — missing elements unique to S.

questions.stem: The shaded region in the Venn diagram represents
questions.explanation: The shading covers the universal set except the lens-shaped overlap between P and Q. That overlap is \(P\cap Q\); shading everything else is its complement, the complement of the intersection, written \((P\cap Q)'\).

distractors.A: \(P\cup Q'\) would shade all of P plus everything outside Q.
distractors.C: \(Q\cap P'\) would shade only the part of Q outside P.
distractors.D: \((P\cup Q)'\) would shade only the area outside both circles, leaving the P-only and Q-only crescents unshaded — but those are shaded here.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram showing sets P and Q with shaded region

questions.stem: M = {p, q, r} and N = {p, q}. Which of the following statements about sets M and N is true?
questions.explanation: Every element of N is already in M, so N is a subset of M. Therefore M ∪ N just gives back M.

distractors.A: It's the other way round: N ⊂ M, not M ⊂ N.
distractors.C: M ∪ N = N would only be true if M ⊂ N, but r is not in N.
distractors.D: M ∩ N = {p, q}, not the empty set.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the universal set U and two sets, T and C, that represent the students in a class who play tennis (T) and chess (C). How many students play tennis but NOT chess?
questions.explanation: "Tennis but NOT chess" means inside the T circle but OUTSIDE the C circle. From the diagram those are Aly, Alex, Tom and Kayla — 4 students. (Paul and Rema are inside C, so excluded; Dora, Doug and Chaz are outside T altogether.)

distractors.A: Two is too few — count Aly, Alex, Tom AND Kayla.
distractors.B: Close, but four — there are four names between the T and C circles.
distractors.D: Six counts every student in T (including Paul + Rema inside C). The question excludes chess-players.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram with named students placed in tennis (T) and chess (C) sets

questions.stem: For every sale amounting to $1, a salesman is paid $0.10 as commission. If his sales for a particular month was $1 020.00, how much commission was he paid?
questions.explanation: Commission = 10% of sales = $1 020.00 × 0.10 = $102.00.

distractors.D: $110 is just an over-rounded estimate.

questions.stem: 3x² × 2x³ =
questions.explanation: Multiply coefficients (3 × 2 = 6) and add the indices (x² × x³ = x⁵). Result: 6x⁵.

distractors.B: Adds the coefficients (3 + 2 = 5) instead of multiplying.
distractors.C: Multiplies the indices (2 × 3 = 6) instead of adding.
distractors.D: Both errors at once: 3 × 2 × 12 — nonsense combination.

questions.stem: Seven times the product of two numbers, a and b, may be written as
questions.explanation: 'Product of a and b' = ab. 'Seven times' that = 7ab.

distractors.A: ab/7 is the product divided by 7, not multiplied.
distractors.C: 7a + b applies 7 only to a, not the whole product.
distractors.D: 7(a + b) uses the sum, not the product.

questions.stem: Given that 3 * 6 = 3 and 2 * 4 = 0, then a * b may be defined as
questions.explanation: Test option A on each pair: 3² − 6 = 9 − 6 = 3 ✓ and 2² − 4 = 4 − 4 = 0 ✓. Both work, so a * b = a² − b.

questions.stem: If \(x = -2\), \(y = 3\), \(t = 3\), then \(\left(\dfrac{x}{y}\right)^{t} = \)
questions.explanation: \(\left(\dfrac{-2}{3}\right)^{3} = \dfrac{(-2)^3}{3^3} = \dfrac{-8}{27}\). The negative cubes to negative.

distractors.C: Forgot the negative sign — odd power preserves sign.
distractors.D: You inverted the fraction.

questions.stem: If \(\dfrac{1}{3^{2x}} = 9\), then the value of \(x\) is
questions.explanation: Rewrite: 1/32x = 3-2x. So 3-2x = 9 = 3². Equating powers: -2x = 2 → x = -1.

distractors.D: x = 1½ gives 1/27.

questions.stem: In parallelogram WXYZ, XY = u and XW = v. R is the midpoint of YZ. RW, expressed in terms of u and v, is
questions.explanation: YZ is parallel to XW so YZ = v. R is the midpoint of YZ, so YR = ½v. From R go to Y then to W: RW = -½v + YW. Since YW = -u + v, we get RW = -½v + (-u + v) = ½v − u.

distractors.B: u + ½v has the wrong sign on u (heading the wrong way).
distractors.C: u − ½v reverses the v component as well.
distractors.D: -½v − u has the wrong sign on the v component.

diagram.alt: Parallelogram WXYZ with vectors u (XY) and v (XW), R as midpoint of YZ

questions.stem: The length of the edge of a cube that has a volume of 27 cm³ is
questions.explanation: Volume of a cube = side³, so side = ∛27 = 3 cm.

distractors.C: 6 cm comes from halving 27 ÷ 4.5 — wrong operation.
distractors.D: 9 cm gives a volume of 729 cm³.

questions.stem: A trapezium PQRS has parallel sides 8 cm and 18 cm and an area of 65 cm². The height, h, of PQRS is
questions.explanation: Area = ½(a + b)h, so 65 = ½(8 + 18)h = 13h. Therefore h = 65 ÷ 13 = 5 cm.

distractors.A: 2.5 cm halves the correct answer (forgot the ½ factor cancels).
distractors.B: 3.6 cm comes from 65 ÷ 18, using only one parallel side.
distractors.D: 8.0 cm comes from 65 ÷ 8, using only the other parallel side.

diagram.alt: Trapezium PQRS with parallel sides 8 cm and 18 cm

questions.stem: A sector \(AOB\) of a circle has angle \(AOB = 60°\) and radius \(OB = r\) units. The length of the arc \(AB\) is
questions.explanation: Arc length \(= \dfrac{\theta}{360°} \times 2\pi r = \dfrac{60}{360}\times 2\pi r = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r\).

distractors.A: \(\dfrac{\pi r}{6}\) uses 30° instead of 60° (or forgets to double).
distractors.D: \(\pi r\) would correspond to 180° — half the circumference.

diagram.alt: Sector AOB of a circle with angle 60° and radius r

questions.stem: A compound shape is made up of a rectangle (width 15 cm) and two identical semi-circles, one on either of the short sides. The total length of the shape is 27 cm. The area of the compound shape, in terms of π, is
questions.explanation: Two semicircles' diameter = 27 − 15 = 12, so radius = 6 (this is also the height of the rectangle). Rectangle = 15 × 12 = 180. Two semicircles = full circle = π(6)² = 36π. Total = 180 + 36π = 36(5 + π).

distractors.A: 6(9 + π) uses the wrong dimensions for the rectangle.
distractors.C: 6(5 + 2π) misuses the radius and circle area.
distractors.D: 18(5 + 2π) doubles up the circle area incorrectly.

diagram.alt: Compound shape: rectangle of width 15 cm with two identical semicircles

questions.subtopic: Effect on area of changing dimensions
questions.stem: The area of a rectangle is 40 cm². If the length is multiplied by 4 and the width is halved, the area would then be
questions.explanation: New area = (4L) × (W/2) = 2 × (LW) = 2 × 40 = 80 cm².

distractors.A: 20 cm² halves the area (only the width change applied).
distractors.B: 40 cm² assumes the changes cancel exactly — they don't.
distractors.D: 160 cm² multiplies by 4 only (ignores the halving).

questions.stem: A compound shape comprises a triangle resting on a square of side 5 cm. If the TOTAL area of the compound shape is 35 cm², then the height of the triangle is
questions.explanation: Square area = 5 × 5 = 25 cm². Triangle area = 35 − 25 = 10 cm². With base 5: ½ × 5 × h = 10, so h = 4 cm.

distractors.A: 2 cm forgets the ½ factor in the triangle's area formula.
distractors.D: 10 cm uses the wrong area for the triangle.

diagram.alt: Compound shape: triangle resting on a square of side 5 cm

questions.stem: A frequency distribution gives mass classes 21–30 (28), 31–40 (34), 41–50 (12), 51–60 (38), 61–70 (20). The number of children who have a mass of at least 41 kg is
questions.explanation: 'At least 41 kg' = sum of the last three classes: 12 + 38 + 20 = 70.

distractors.D: 104 adds another class by mistake.

questions.stem: Using the same frequency distribution as in the previous item — mass classes 21–30 (28), 31–40 (34), 41–50 (12), 51–60 (38), 61–70 (20) — the lower boundary of the modal class is
questions.explanation: The modal class has the highest frequency, which is 38 in the 51–60 class. The lower class boundary is 50.5 (halfway between 50 and 51).

distractors.A: 30.5 is the lower boundary of the 31–40 class.
distractors.B: \(41.0\) is the lower limit of the 41–50 class, not the modal class boundary.
distractors.D: \(51.0\) is the lower class limit; the lower boundary sits halfway between 50 and 51.

questions.stem: A two-way table records 100 respondents by ethnicity (P or R) and attitude towards Mathematics (Positive, Neutral, Negative). The cells are P–Positive 25, R–Positive 12; P–Neutral 11, R–Neutral 9; P–Negative 24, R–Negative 19. A respondent is selected at random. What is the probability that the selected respondent is of ethnicity P and has a positive attitude towards Mathematics?
questions.explanation: Required outcomes = 25 (cell where P meets Positive). Total outcomes = 100. P(P and Positive) = 25/100 = 0.25.

distractors.B: 0.37 is the probability of being Positive overall (37/100).
distractors.D: 0.60 is the probability of being ethnicity P (60/100).

questions.stem: Which of the following diagrams BEST illustrates a function?

options.A: Mapping where one input is linked to several outputs
options.B: Mapping where each input has exactly one output (one-to-one)
options.C: Mapping where one input has two outputs
options.D: Mapping where two inputs share an output but one input also branches

questions.explanation: A function assigns exactly one output to each input. The one-to-one mapping in option B satisfies this without ambiguity. Options A and C show inputs with multiple outputs, which violates the definition.

distractors.A: An input mapping to several outputs is not a function.
distractors.C: Same problem — one input gives more than one output.
distractors.D: Branching from any single input also breaks the function rule.

questions.stem: The graph below shows a straight line AB. The point where the line AB crosses the horizontal axis is
questions.explanation: The line crosses the horizontal (x) axis where y = 0. From the graph that point is (3, 0).

distractors.B: (0, 3) is on the y-axis, not the x-axis.
distractors.C: (0, -2) is the y-intercept (the other end of segment AB).
distractors.D: (3, -2) isn't on either axis.

diagram.alt: Line AB on a coordinate plane — passes through A(0, -2) and B(3, 0)

questions.stem: For the same straight line AB shown in the previous item, the equation of AB is
questions.explanation: Gradient = (0 − (−2)) / (3 − 0) = 2/3. The y-intercept is −2. So y = (2/3)x − 2.

distractors.A: Slope of 1 wouldn't pass through both A and B.
distractors.B: Slope of 2 puts B at y = 4 instead of 0.
distractors.D: Negative slope would slope the wrong way.

diagram.alt: Line AB on a coordinate plane — passes through A(0, -2) and B(3, 0)

questions.stem: The coordinates of the maximum point of the function y = 4x − x² are
questions.explanation: Complete the square: y = -(x² − 4x) = -(x − 2)² + 4. Maximum occurs at x = 2, giving y = 4. So max point is (2, 4).

distractors.B: (0, 4) is the y-intercept value but x = 0 gives y = 0, not 4.
distractors.D: (4, 4) plugs x = 4 to get y = 0, not 4.

questions.stem: The equation of the axis of symmetry of the quadratic function y = 4x − x² is
questions.explanation: The axis of symmetry passes vertically through the maximum point (2, 4). Its equation is x = 2.

distractors.B: y = 2 is a horizontal line — axis of symmetry of a parabola is vertical.
distractors.C: y = 4 is the horizontal line through the vertex, not the axis.
distractors.D: y = 2x isn't even a constant line.

distractors.B: -1 comes from forgetting the minus on 14 − 3 step.
distractors.D: 13/5 = (14 − 3)/5 — drops the negative.

questions.stem: A parking lot charges $1.00 plus $0.25 for every hour or part thereof (h) in which a vehicle is parked. A function, C(h), that represents the parking cost is
questions.explanation: Fixed charge $1.00 + variable charge $0.25 per hour: C(h) = 0.25h + 1.00.

distractors.B: Swaps the roles of the per-hour and flat charges.
distractors.D: Dividing by 0.25 doesn't represent a per-hour charge.

questions.stem: If the sum of the interior angles of a polygon is 4 right angles, then the polygon is a
questions.explanation: 4 right angles = 360°. Using (n − 2)·180° = 360° gives n − 2 = 2, so n = 4 (quadrilateral).

distractors.A: Triangle's angles sum to 180° (2 right angles).

questions.stem: In a right-angled triangle with right angle at B, BC = 4 cm (vertical leg) and CA = 8 cm (hypotenuse). Angle x is at A and angle y is at C. Which trigonometric ratio is equal to 4/8?
questions.explanation: From angle x (at vertex A): the side opposite is BC = 4 and the hypotenuse is CA = 8. So sin x = opposite/hypotenuse = 4/8.

distractors.B: Tan y uses opposite/adjacent at C — neither side here is 8.
distractors.C: Sin y = opposite/hyp = AB/8, where AB ≠ 4.
distractors.D: Tan x = opposite/adjacent = 4/AB, not 4/8.

diagram.alt: Right-angled triangle with right angle at B, BC = 4 cm, CA = 8 cm

questions.stem: Two right-angled triangles are similar. Triangle ABC has angle 31° at A, AB = 6 cm and hypotenuse AC = 7 cm. Triangle MNO has angle 31° at M and MN = 3 cm. The length of MO, in cm, is
questions.explanation: The triangles are similar with scale factor MN/AB = 3/6 = ½. MO corresponds to AC, so MO = 7 × ½ = 3.5 cm.

distractors.D: 7.0 keeps the original length, ignoring the scale.

diagram.alt: Two similar right-angled triangles ABC and PQR

questions.stem: A(2, 5) is mapped onto A'(4, 6) and B(5, 7) is mapped onto B'(7, 8). The translation vector is
questions.explanation: The translation vector is the change in position from object to image: \(\begin{pmatrix}4-2\\6-5\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix}\). The same vector maps \(B\) onto \(B'\): \(\begin{pmatrix}7-5\\8-7\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix}\).

distractors.D: (5, 3) would shift A to (7, 8).

diagram.alt: Translation on coordinate plane mapping A and B onto A′ and B′

questions.stem: OAA', OBB' and OCC' are straight lines. Triangle ABC is mapped onto triangle A'B'C' by an enlargement with centre O. What is the scale factor of the enlargement? (See the diagram below.)
questions.explanation: The image is on the same side of O as the object and is twice as far away, so the scale factor is +2.

distractors.A: Negative would flip the image to the other side of O.
distractors.B: -½ flips and shrinks — the image here is larger.
distractors.C: +½ would shrink the image; here it's enlarged.

diagram.alt: Enlargement of triangle ABC onto A'B'C' with OAA', OBB' and OCC' as straight lines

questions.stem: The angle of depression of a point G from E is 30°. F is directly below E with EF = 25 m, and FG is horizontal (right angle at F). The distance FG, in metres, is
questions.explanation: In right triangle EFG (right angle at F), the angle at E equals the angle of depression's complement = 60°. tan(60°) = FG/25, so FG = 25 tan 60°. Since tan 60° = 1/tan 30°, this equals 25 / tan 30°.

distractors.B: 25/tan 60° = 25/√3, which is the height divided by tan 60° — wrong angle used.
distractors.C: 25 cos 30° gives the adjacent side from a different triangle setup.
distractors.D: 25 sin 30° = 12.5 — too short for FG.

diagram.alt: Angle of depression of point G from E with F directly below E, EF = 25 m

questions.stem: A surveyor sets up his instrument 12 metres from the foot of a building. The instrument is 1.6 m above the ground and the angle of elevation to the top of the building is 40°. An estimate of the height of the building can be obtained by calculating
questions.explanation: From instrument level: rise of building above instrument = 12 tan 40°. The instrument itself is 1.6 m above the ground, so total height = 1.6 + 12 tan 40°.

distractors.B: Cosine gives the adjacent side, not the vertical rise.
distractors.C: Subtracting 1.6 ignores that the instrument adds height to the ground level.
distractors.D: Sine on the adjacent leg is the wrong ratio entirely.

diagram.alt: Surveyor with instrument 12 m from building, instrument height above ground

questions.stem: The value of the digit 2 in the number 748.621 is
questions.explanation: In 748.621, the digit 2 sits in the hundredths place — the second position after the decimal point. Its value is \(\dfrac{2}{100} = \dfrac{2}{10^{2}}\).

distractors.B: \(\dfrac{2}{10^{3}} = 0.002\) — that's the thousandths place, where the 1 sits.
distractors.C: \(2\times 10^{-1} = 0.2\) — the tenths place, where the 6 sits.
distractors.D: \(2000\times 10^{-3} = 2\) — that would put the digit in the units column.

distractors.A: $1 would come from \(\sqrt{17-15}\cdot\sqrt{17+15}\) taken as \(\sqrt{2-2}\) — sign mistake.
distractors.B: $2 is just $17-15, forgetting the squaring and the square root.
distractors.D: $16 would be \(\sqrt{256}\) — perhaps \(17^{2}-15^{2}\) mis-evaluated as $32 then doubled.

questions.stem: In which of the following pairs of sets is one set NOT a subset of the other?

options.D: G = {multiples of five} and H = {multiples of ten}

questions.explanation:
Check each pair:

  • (A) {multiples of 12} = {12, 24, 36,.} and {factors of 8} = {1, 2, 4, 8}. Neither is a subset of the other (no common elements).
  • (B) {factors of 6} = {1, 2, 3, 6} ⊂ {factors of 12} = {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12}.
  • (C) Every whole number is a rational number, so {whole numbers} ⊂ {rational numbers}.
  • (D) Every multiple of 10 is also a multiple of 5, so {multiples of 10} ⊂ {multiples of 5}.

Only pair A has neither set being a subset of the other.

distractors.B: {factors of 6} ⊂ {factors of 12} since every factor of 6 is also a factor of 12.
distractors.C: Whole numbers are a subset of rational numbers (any integer can be written as a fraction).
distractors.D: Multiples of 10 are a subset of multiples of 5.

questions.stem: All students in a class play Scrabble or Checkers or both. Sixteen per cent of the students play BOTH Scrabble and Checkers. The number of students who play Checkers only is TWICE the number of students who play Scrabble only. What percentage of students play Checkers but NOT Scrabble?
questions.explanation: Let Scrabble only = \(s\). Then Checkers only = \(2s\). Since everyone plays at least one game: \(s + 2s + 16 = 100\), so \(3s = 84\) and \(s = 28\). Therefore Checkers only \(= 2(28) = 56\%\).

distractors.A: \(28\%\) is Scrabble only (not what was asked).
distractors.D: \(72\%\) is the total who play Checkers (both + Checkers only = \(16 + 56 = 72\)), which is more than just 'Checkers but NOT Scrabble'.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a Venn diagram where U = {students who play games}, H = {students who play hockey}, V = {students who play volleyball}, with the number of students in each region indicated. How many students play EITHER hockey OR volleyball but NOT both?
questions.explanation: 'EITHER hockey OR volleyball but NOT both' is the symmetric difference: count the students in H only plus the students in V only (excluding the overlap). From the diagram this gives 9.

distractors.A: $6 counts only one of the two 'only' regions.
distractors.B: $7 misses one student from a region.
distractors.D: $11 adds the intersection to one of the 'only' regions.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram for hockey and volleyball with student counts

questions.stem: A shopping bill shows: 4 kg flour at unit price P with total $12.40; 2 kg sugar at $3.60 per kg with total $7.20; 8 kg rice at unit price Q with total T; subtotal $40.40. The correct values of P, Q and T are
questions.explanation: P (unit price of flour) = $12.40 ÷ 4 = $3.10. Subtotal of rice = $40.40 − $12.40 − $7.20 = $20.80, so T = $20.80. Q (unit price of rice) = $20.80 ÷ 8 = $2.60.

distractors.A: These values don't add up to the subtotal of $40.40.
distractors.B: Q = $6.70 would give rice total = 8 × 6.70 = $53.60 — way over.
distractors.C: P = $4.05 would make flour total $16.20, not $12.40.

diagram.alt: Shopping bill table — items 13 and 14 share this diagram

questions.stem: On a shopping bill with a subtotal of \(\$40.50\), the amount paid for VAT charged at \(8\%\) is

distractors.A: \(\$3.10\) is roughly \(7.66\%\) of the subtotal — slightly under \(8\%\).
distractors.C: \(\$37.26\) is the subtotal after the VAT is removed (\(\$40.50-\$3.24\)).
distractors.D: \(\$43.74\) is the total bill including VAT, not the VAT itself.

questions.stem: By selling a bag for $1 140, Vishal incurred a loss of 5%. At what price should he have sold the bag to gain a profit of 5%?
questions.explanation: Cost price: 0.95 × CP = $1 140, so CP = $1 200. To gain 5%: SP = 1.05 × $1 200 = $1 260.00.

distractors.A: $1 197.00 = $1 140 × 1.05 — applies 5% on the loss-making price, not the cost price.

questions.stem: The cost of a machine is estimated to be decreasing at the rate of 10% every year. If it currently costs $6 000, what will be the estimated value of the machine after 2 years?
questions.explanation: Each year the value is multiplied by 0.90. After 2 years: $6 000 × 0.90² = $6 000 × 0.81 = $4 860.

distractors.A: $1 140 is far too low — applies depreciation incorrectly.
distractors.B: $4 800 = $6 000 × 0.80 — combines the two years' losses as 20% once.
distractors.D: $5 400 = $6 000 × 0.90 — only one year's depreciation.

questions.stem: The compound interest on $12 000 for 2 years at 10% per annum, compounded annually, is
questions.explanation: Amount after 2 years = $12 000 × 1.10² = $12 000 × 1.21 = $14 520. Compound interest = $14 520 − $12 000 = $2 520.

distractors.D: $14 520 is the final amount, not just the interest.

questions.stem: The product of a number \(p\) and its reciprocal may be written as
questions.explanation: The reciprocal of \(p\) is \(\dfrac{1}{p}\). The product of \(p\) and its reciprocal is \(p\times \dfrac{1}{p} = 1\).

distractors.B: \(p\times (-p) = -p^{2}\) — uses the negative, not the reciprocal.
distractors.C: \(p^{2}\times \dfrac{1}{p} = p\) — uses the reciprocal but wrong first factor.

questions.stem: Althea, Bob and Chris collect shells. Althea has \(p\) shells, Bob has TWICE as many shells as Althea, and Chris has 4 more than the total number of shells that both Althea and Bob collected. Altogether, the total number of shells they have is

distractors.A: \(6p\) misses the constant \(+4\) from Chris's count.
distractors.B: \(3p+4\) is just Chris's count, not the total.

questions.stem: The value of \(5^{n+1}\times 5^{n+2}\) when \(n=-1\) is

distractors.C: $10 doesn't arise from a power of 5.

questions.stem: If 40 students will take 20 days to paint a wall, how many days will it take to paint the wall if 10 more students are added, working at the same rate?
questions.explanation: This is inverse proportion: students \(\times\) days \(=\) constant. \(40\times 20 = 50\times d\), so \(d = \dfrac{800}{50} = 16\) days.

distractors.A: $12 days uses \(\frac{800}{60}\approx 13.3\) — wrong number of students.
distractors.B: $15 comes from \(\frac{800}{50}\approx 15\) rounded down — but the exact answer is 16.
distractors.D: $18 is just $20-2 for the 'extra two' students misapplied.

questions.stem: If \(P = \begin{pmatrix}2 & 5\\ -1 & 3\end{pmatrix}\), then the value of \(|P|\) is

distractors.B: $1 comes from \(6 - 5 = 1\) — sign error on the cross-product term.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a parallelogram EFGH in which EF is parallel to HG, EH is parallel to FG, EF = k and EH = m. EG expressed in terms of k and m is
questions.explanation: From E, go to F (vector \(k\)), then from F to G (which is parallel to EH and equal in length, so vector \(m\)). \(\vec{EG} = \vec{EF} + \vec{FG} = k + m\).

diagram.alt: Parallelogram EFGH with vectors EF = k and EH = m

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a cylinder whose diameter is 6 cm and height 21 cm. Given that \(\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}\), the volume of the cylinder, in cm³, is
questions.explanation: Radius \(= 3\) cm. Volume \(= \pi r^{2}h = \dfrac{22}{7}\times 3^{2}\times 21 = \dfrac{22}{7}\times 9\times 21 = 22\times 9\times 3 = 594\) cm³.

distractors.B: \(396 = \pi\times 6\times 21\) — uses the diameter as \(r\) but only to power 1.
distractors.D: \(2\,376 = 4\times 594\) — uses diameter $6 as the radius.

diagram.alt: Cylinder with labelled height and radius

questions.stem: In a rectangular garden plot, 15 m long and 12 m wide, an area of 80 m² is used for a vegetable garden. What area of the plot is NOT used for vegetable gardening?
questions.explanation: Total area \(= 15\times 12 = 180\) m². Area not used \(= 180 - 80 = 100\) m².

distractors.A: $26 m² takes the perimeter instead of the area.
distractors.C: $134 m² doesn't match a clean subtraction.
distractors.D: $260 m² doubles the unused area incorrectly.

questions.stem: How long will a speedboat take to travel between two harbours which are 1 080 km apart, if it travels at an average speed of 120 km h⁻¹?
questions.explanation: Time \(= \dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} = \dfrac{1080}{120} = 9\) hours.

distractors.A: $8 hours \(\times 120 = 960\) km — short of the distance.
distractors.D: $15 hours \(\times 120 = 1800\) km — far too long.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a circle, centred at O, with a major sector of area 115.5 cm² and a minor arc subtending a right angle at O. (Use \(\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}\).) The diameter of the circle, in cm, is
questions.explanation: The minor sector subtends $90°, so the major sector subtends $270° — that is \(\dfrac{3}{4}\) of the circle. So \(\dfrac{3}{4}\pi r^{2} = 115.5\), giving \(\pi r^{2} = 154\). Then \(r^{2} = 154\div \dfrac{22}{7} = 154\times \dfrac{7}{22} = 49\), so \(r = 7\) cm. Diameter \(= 14\) cm.

distractors.B: $7.0 cm is the radius, not the diameter.
distractors.D: $28.0 cm doubles the diameter.

diagram.alt: Circle showing major and minor sectors

questions.subtopic: Surface area of a compound solid
questions.stem: The diagram below shows a compound solid: a rectangular prism of dimensions 12 cm by 10 cm by 10 cm, with a triangular prism roof of slant 13 cm and vertical face 8 cm on the left, depth 10 cm. The surface area of the compound solid, in cm², is
questions.explanation: The compound solid combines a rectangular base and a triangular prism roof. Adding the visible surfaces (two pentagonal sides, the rectangular front, slanted top, vertical back-top, and bottom) gives a total surface area of $866 cm².

distractors.A: $746 misses one of the rectangular faces.
distractors.D: \(1\,100\) overcounts the top region.

diagram.alt: Compound solid: cuboid with triangular-prism roof

questions.stem: The distance around a lake is 8 km. On a map, this distance around the lake is represented by a length of 4 cm. The scale on the map is

distractors.B: \(1:2\,000\) converts km to metres but not to cm.

questions.stem: The table below shows the number of words that a group of 60 students got correct in a spelling test of 10 words: 6 words → 3 students, 7 words → 18 students, 8 words → 7 students, 9 words → 16 students, 10 words → 16 students. The mode of the number of words spelt correctly is
questions.explanation: The mode is the value with the highest frequency. The highest frequency is 18 (for 7 words). So the mode is 7.

distractors.C: $16 is a frequency, not a value.
distractors.D: $18 is the highest frequency, but the mode is the value with that frequency, which is 7.

diagram.alt: Frequency table of words spelt correctly (used by Q39 and Q40)

questions.stem: Using the same frequency distribution as the previous item (60 students; 6 words → 3, 7 words → 18, 8 words → 7, 9 words → 16, 10 words → 16), the median number of words the students spelt correctly during the test is
questions.explanation: For 60 students, the median is the average of the 30th and 31st values. Cumulative frequencies: 6→3, 7→21, 8→28, 9→44, 10→60. Both the 30th and 31st values fall in the '9 words' class. Median = 9.

distractors.A: $8 — only 28 students scored 8 or fewer; the 30th student is in the next group.
distractors.C: $16 is a frequency, not a value.
distractors.D: $18 is a frequency, not a value.

diagram.alt: Frequency table of words spelt correctly (used by Q39 and Q40)

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the cumulative frequency curve of the heights of 20 seedlings. The semi-interquartile range of the set of measurements is
questions.explanation: For 20 seedlings: \(Q_{1}\) (lower quartile) is the height at cumulative frequency \(\dfrac{20}{4}=5\) — reading the curve gives \(\approx 1.30\) cm. \(Q_{3}\) (upper quartile) is the height at cumulative frequency \(\dfrac{3\times 20}{4}=15\) — reading the curve gives \(\approx 2.60\) cm. Semi-interquartile range \(=\dfrac{Q_{3}-Q_{1}}{2}=\dfrac{2.60-1.30}{2}=0.65\) cm.

distractors.B: $1.30 cm is the interquartile range \(Q_{3}-Q_{1}\) itself, not the semi-IQR.
distractors.C: $2.00 cm doesn't match the curve readings.
distractors.D: $2.60 cm doubles the IQR.

diagram.alt: Cumulative frequency curve of seedling heights

questions.subtopic: Solutions of a quadratic from a graph
questions.stem: According to the graph of \(y = 4x - x^{2}\), the solutions of the simultaneous equations \(y = 4x - x^{2}\) and \(y = 0\) are

options.A: x = 0 when y = 0 and x = 4 when y = 0
options.B: x = 0 when y = 0 and x = 2 when y = 4
options.C: x = 2 when y = 4 and x = 4 when y = 0
options.D: x = 0 when y = 0 and x = −4 when y = −32

distractors.B: \((2, 4)\) is the maximum point of the parabola, not a y = 0 intersection.
distractors.C: Same issue — \((2, 4)\) is the vertex, not where the curve meets the x-axis.
distractors.D: \(x = -4\) gives \(y = 4(-4) - (-4)^{2} = -16 - 16 = -32\) — that point is on the curve but it's not where y = 0.

diagram.alt: Quadratic graph

questions.stem: If \(h(x) = 4x^{2} - 6\), then \(h\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \)

distractors.A: \(-7\) would come from \(4(-1/2) - 6 = -8\) — forgot to square.
distractors.D: $5 has the wrong sign.

questions.stem: A line \(L\) is perpendicular to the line \(2x - y - 8 = 0\). What is the gradient of the line \(L\)?
questions.explanation: Rearrange to slope-intercept form: \(y = 2x - 8\). Gradient of given line is 2. Perpendicular gradient \(= -\dfrac{1}{2}\) (negative reciprocal).

distractors.A: \(-2\) flips the sign but not the reciprocal.
distractors.C: \(\dfrac{1}{2}\) is the reciprocal but missing the negative.
distractors.D: $2 is the original gradient.

questions.subtopic: Angle at the centre and circumference
questions.stem: The diagram below shows a circle with its centre at O. Angle POQ = 122°, and the value y° is at S (a point on the circle). The value of y° is
questions.explanation: The angle at the centre (\(\angle POQ = 122°\)) is twice the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc PQ. So \(y = \dfrac{122}{2} = 61°\).

distractors.B: $58° would require the central angle to be $116°.
distractors.D: $84° doesn't match the centre/circumference relationship.

diagram.alt: Circle with angles subtended at the centre

questions.stem: The diagram below shows two parallel lines PQ and RS cut by a transversal, with alternate interior angles labelled \(x\) and \(y\). Which of the following BEST describes the relation between \(x\) and \(y\)?
questions.explanation: \(x\) and \(y\) are alternate interior angles — on opposite sides of the transversal, both between the two parallel lines. When the lines are parallel, alternate interior angles are equal: \(x = y\).

distractors.B: There's no reason for one to be smaller — equal alternate angles is the rule.
distractors.C: \(x + y = 90°\) would only be true for complementary angles, not alternate angles.
distractors.D: \(x + y = 180°\) is the rule for co-interior (allied) angles — the SAME side of the transversal. These are on opposite sides, so they're alternate (not co-interior).

diagram.alt: Parallel lines cut by a transversal with points R and S

questions.stem: The diagram below shows triangle ABC and its images A'B'C' and A''B''C'' after triangle ABC undergoes a composite (double) transformation. What sequence of transformations will map triangle ABC onto its image triangle A''B''C''?

options.A: A reflection in the line \(x = 1\), followed by a translation of \(\begin{pmatrix}-3\\ -4\end{pmatrix}\)
options.B: A translation of \(\begin{pmatrix}-3\\ -4\end{pmatrix}\) followed by a reflection in the x-axis
options.C: A translation of 4 units to the left, followed by a clockwise rotation of 90° about the origin
options.D: A counterclockwise rotation of 90° about the origin followed by a translation of 4 units downwards

questions.explanation: Reflecting triangle ABC in the vertical line \(x = 1\) produces A'B'C' on the right side. Then translating each point by \(\begin{pmatrix}-3\\ -4\end{pmatrix}\) moves it to A''B''C'' below.

distractors.B: Translation first then reflection in x-axis would give the wrong final orientation and position.
distractors.C: A clockwise rotation about the origin would not produce the observed image positions.
distractors.D: A counterclockwise rotation followed by a downward translation does not match the geometry.

diagram.alt: Composite transformation mapping triangle ABC to A″B″C″

questions.stem: In each of the following diagrams, A' is the image of A. Which of the diagrams shows a reflection in the x-axis?
questions.explanation: A reflection in the x-axis takes \((x, y)\) to \((x, -y)\). The image must sit directly below the object at the same x-coordinate. Diagram D shows exactly this — A' is the mirror image of A through the x-axis.

distractors.A: Diagram A shows a rotation about the origin (or reflection in \(y = -x\)).
distractors.B: Diagram B shows a reflection in the y-axis (\(A\) and \(A'\) at the same height).
distractors.C: Diagram C shows a 90° clockwise rotation about the origin — not a reflection.

questions.stem: In triangle ABC, angle A = \(x°\) and angle B = \(2x°\). What is the size of angle C?
questions.explanation: Angles in a triangle sum to $180°: \(x + 2x + C = 180\), so \(C = 180 - 3x\) degrees.

distractors.C: $30° would only be true for a specific value of \(x\).
distractors.D: \(\dfrac{180}{3x}\) divides instead of subtracting — that's the wrong operation.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the angle of elevation of a point Z from X is 35°. If X is 15 metres from Z (along the line of sight), then the height YZ, in metres, is
questions.explanation: In the right triangle X–Y–Z (right angle at Y), with the 35° angle at X and the hypotenuse XZ = 15 m, the height YZ is the side opposite the 35° angle. So YZ = 15 × sin 35° → option C.

distractors.A: 15 cos 35° gives the horizontal distance XY (adjacent), not the vertical height.
distractors.B: 15 sin 55° equals 15 cos 35° — same horizontal distance, not height.
distractors.D: 15 tan 55° applies tan to the wrong reference angle and to the hypotenuse instead of the adjacent side.

diagram.alt: Right triangle with vertices X, Y, Z — angle of elevation 35° at X, hypotenuse XZ = 15 m

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a pair of similar triangles. Triangle ABC has a right angle at B with AB = 6 cm, BC = 7 cm and angle A = 31° (angle C = 59°). Triangle MNO has a right angle at N with MN = 3 cm and the same 31° angle at M. The length of MO, in centimetres, is
questions.explanation: The triangles are similar with scale factor \(\dfrac{MN}{AB} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}\). MO corresponds to AC (the hypotenuse of triangle ABC). Wait — looking at the diagram, MO likely corresponds to BC (which is 7 cm). MO \(= 7\times \dfrac{1}{2} = 3.5\) cm.

distractors.C: $4.6 cm doesn't match the scale factor.
distractors.D: $6.0 cm matches AB (the corresponding side in the larger triangle).

diagram.alt: Two similar triangles ABC and PQR

questions.stem: What percentage of \(40\) is \(50\)?
questions.explanation: \(\dfrac{50}{40}\times 100\% = \dfrac{5}{4}\times 100\% = 125\%\). The result is over 100% because 50 is larger than 40.

distractors.A: \(20\%\) flips the question to '40 is what % of 200'.
distractors.B: \(25\%\) is \(\frac{50-40}{40}\) — the percentage increase, not what % of 40 is 50.
distractors.C: \(80\%\) is \(\dfrac{40}{50}\times 100\%\) — what % of 50 is 40 (the inverse question).

distractors.B: Off by a factor of 10 — divides by 100 instead of 1 000.
distractors.D: Same as the original (no division at all).

questions.stem: Dan sold a certain number of concert tickets in 3 days. The ratio of the number of tickets sold over the 3 days is \(3:7:5\) respectively. If he sold 8 MORE tickets on Day 2 than on Day 3, the number of tickets he sold ALTOGETHER is
questions.explanation:
Let one part \(=x\). Day 2 has \(7x\) and Day 3 has \(5x\), so \(7x-5x=2x=8\), giving \(x=4\).

Total parts \(=3+7+5=15\), so total tickets \(=15\times 4=60\).

distractors.A: \(40\) comes from forgetting Day 1 — only counting Day 2 + Day 3 = \(7(4)+5(4)=48\), but rounded.
distractors.D: \(120\) takes \(x=8\) directly instead of solving \(2x=8\).

questions.stem: Which of the following sets is defined by \(\{x\in \mathbb{Z}: -2 < x < 4\}\)?
questions.explanation: The strict inequalities \(-2

distractors.A: Includes 4 (which is excluded by strict \(<\)) and starts at 1, missing 0 and the negative.
distractors.B: Includes 4 (which is excluded by strict \(<\)) and misses \(-1\).
distractors.D: Includes both endpoints \(-2\) and \(4\) — that would be \(\le\), not strict \(<\).

questions.explanation:
By the inclusion–exclusion rule: \(n(A\cup B)=n(A)+n(B)-n(A\cap B)=14+15-6=23\).

The complement of \(A\cup B\) is everything in the universe NOT in \(A\cup B\): \(n((A\cup B)')=n(U)-n(A\cup B)=25-23=2\).

distractors.D: \(23=n(A\cup B)\) itself, before taking the complement.

questions.stem: The Venn diagram below shows the universal set U of 9 students, with T the set who play tennis and C the set who play chess (C ⊂ T). T contains Aly, Tom, Kayla, Paul and Rema; C contains Paul and Rema only. Outside T are Dora, Alex, Doug and Chaz. (Note: with C ⊂ T, every chess-player also plays tennis.) Considering the corrected diagram with Alex inside T as well — how many students play tennis but NOT chess?
questions.explanation: "Tennis but NOT chess" = inside T, outside C. From the diagram: Aly, Alex, Tom and Kayla — 4 students.

distractors.A: \(2\) counts only the students inside \(C\) (Paul and Rema).
distractors.C: \(4\) likely counts everyone outside \(C\) (Aly, Tom, Kayla and one extra) — but Dora/Alex/Doug/Chaz are outside both \(T\) and \(C\).
distractors.D: \(6\) counts the students who do NOT play chess (everyone except Paul and Rema), but the question asks for those who play tennis.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram showing universal set U with tennis (T) and chess (C) circles

questions.stem: Given \(Q=\{\text{odd numbers}\}\), \(R=\{\text{even numbers}\}\) and \(P=\{\text{prime numbers}\}\), which of the following sets is empty?
questions.explanation: No integer can be both odd AND even at the same time, so \(Q\cap R=\emptyset\).

distractors.A: \(P\cap R\) contains the prime 2 (the only even prime), so it is not empty.
distractors.B: \(P\cup Q\) contains every odd number plus the prime 2 — far from empty.
distractors.C: \(P\cap Q\) contains every odd prime: \(3,5,7,11,\ldots\) — not empty.

questions.stem: At the end of any year, a computer is worth 4% less than what it was worth at the beginning of the year. If the computer is worth $4 200 in December 2023, then its value in January 2023 was
questions.explanation: End-of-year value is 96% of start-of-year value: \(4\,200 = 0.96\times V\), so \(V=\dfrac{4\,200}{0.96}=\$4\,375\).

distractors.A: \(\$4\,050\) comes from \(4\,200-150\) — wrong base for the 4%.
distractors.B: \(\$4\,032 = 4\,200\times 0.96\) — depreciates a SECOND time instead of reversing the depreciation.
distractors.C: \(\$4\,368 = 4\,200\times 1.04\) — adds 4% of the new value, but the 4% should be of the original (start-of-year) value.

questions.stem: A man's basic pay for a 40-hour week is $480. Overtime is paid at 25% in excess of the basic rate. In a certain week he works overtime and his TOTAL wage is $600. Altogether, the number of hours he works for the week is
questions.explanation:
Basic rate = \(\dfrac{480}{40}=\$12\) per hour. Overtime rate = \(12\times 1.25=\$15\) per hour.

Overtime pay = \(600-480=\$120\), so overtime hours = \(\dfrac{120}{15}=8\). Total hours = \(40+8=48\).

distractors.B: \(47\) splits the $120 into 7 hours at the wrong rate.
distractors.D: \(50\) divides $120 by $12 (basic rate, ignoring the 25% premium).

questions.stem: The sum of seven times the number \(a\) and the number \(b\) may be written as
questions.explanation: Seven times \(a\) is \(7a\). The SUM of \(7a\) and \(b\) is \(7a+b\).

distractors.A: \(7ab\) is the PRODUCT of 7, \(a\) and \(b\) — multiplied, not summed.
distractors.D: \(7(a+b)=7a+7b\) — multiplies the sum by 7, but the 'seven times' applies only to \(a\).

questions.stem: The algebraic fraction \(\dfrac{14a^{2}b^{2}}{42a^{3}b}\) written in its simplest form is
questions.explanation: Cancel: \(\dfrac{14}{42}=\dfrac{1}{3}\), \(\dfrac{a^{2}}{a^{3}}=\dfrac{1}{a}\), and \(\dfrac{b^{2}}{b}=b\). So the fraction simplifies to \(\dfrac{1\cdot b}{3\cdot a}=\dfrac{b}{3a}\).

distractors.B: Inverts numerator and denominator after cancelling.
distractors.C: Multiplies the powers instead of subtracting them.
distractors.D: Multiplies the numerical coefficient and powers — not how fraction simplification works.

questions.stem: The value of \(3^{2x+1}\times \dfrac{1}{9^{x}}\) when \(x=-\dfrac{1}{2}\) is

distractors.B: \(9=3^{2}\) — perhaps from forgetting the \(+1\) in the exponent.
distractors.D: \(27=3^{3}\) — would result if the exponents added incorrectly.

questions.stem: The sum of two numbers is 140. If one number is 10 less than two times the other, then the two numbers are
questions.explanation: Let the smaller number be \(x\) and the other be \(2x-10\). Then \(x+(2x-10)=140\), so \(3x=150\) and \(x=50\). The other number is \(2(50)-10=90\).

distractors.B: Sum is 140 ✓ but does not satisfy '10 less than two times the other'.
distractors.D: \(2(30)-10=50\neq 110\) — fails the relation.

questions.stem: Given that \(2*3=1\) and \(4*7=9\), then \(a*b\) may be defined as

In triangle \(OLM\), \(R\) is the midpoint of \(OL\) and \(T\) is the midpoint of \(OM\). Given that \(\overrightarrow{OR}=\mathbf{a}\) and \(\overrightarrow{OT}=\mathbf{b}\), the vector \(\overrightarrow{TL}\), expressed in terms of \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\), is
questions.explanation: Since \(R\) is the midpoint of \(OL\), \(\overrightarrow{OL}=2\mathbf{a}\). Then \(\overrightarrow{TL}=\overrightarrow{TO}+\overrightarrow{OL}=-\mathbf{b}+2\mathbf{a}=2\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}\).

distractors.A: \(\mathbf{b}+2\mathbf{a}\) misses the negative sign on \(\mathbf{b}\) (going from \(T\) to \(O\) reverses direction).
distractors.C: \(2(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a})\) swaps the roles of the two midpoints.
distractors.D: \(\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})\) is the midpoint of segment \(RT\), not the vector \(\overrightarrow{TL}\).

diagram.alt: Triangle OLM with R as midpoint of OL and T as midpoint of OM, vectors a and b

questions.stem: The matrix \(A=\begin{pmatrix}2 & a\\ b & -2\end{pmatrix}\) maps the point \(P(2,5)\) onto the point \(Q(-1,2)\). The values of \(a\) and \(b\) are
questions.explanation:
Apply \(A\) to \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\5\end{pmatrix}\): \(\begin{pmatrix}2(2)+a(5)\\ b(2)+(-2)(5)\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}4+5a\\ 2b-10\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}-1\\ 2\end{pmatrix}\).

From the first row: \(4+5a=-1\Rightarrow a=-1\). From the second row: \(2b-10=2\Rightarrow b=6\).

distractors.D: Sign error on \(b\) — gives \(-12-10=-22\), not \(2\).

questions.stem: Given that \(1\text{ centimetre}=\dfrac{1}{100\,000}\text{ kilometres}\), then \(2.5\) kilometres, in centimetres, is

distractors.A: \(250\) is \(2.5\times 100\) — converts km to metres only.
distractors.B: \(2\,500\) is \(2.5\times 1\,000\) — gives metres again, off by 100.
distractors.C: \(25\,000\) is off by a factor of 10.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a triangular prism with isosceles triangular cross-section: base 6 cm, slant sides 5 cm each, perpendicular height 4 cm; the length of the prism is 10 cm. The surface area of the triangular prism, in cm\(^{2}\), is
questions.explanation:
Two triangular ends: \(2\times \dfrac{1}{2}\times 6\times 4=24\text{ cm}^{2}\).

Three rectangles: \(6\times 10=60\) (base), \(5\times 10=50\) (left slant), \(5\times 10=50\) (right slant). Sum \(=160\text{ cm}^{2}\).

Total surface area \(=24+160=184\text{ cm}^{2}\).

distractors.D: \(320\) doubles each rectangle (counts both inside and outside).

diagram.alt: Triangular prism with isosceles cross-section (base 6 cm, slant 5 cm, height 4 cm)

questions.stem: If the radius of a circular pond, in terms of \(\pi\), is \(\dfrac{44}{\pi}\) metres, then the distance around the edge of the circular pond, in metres, is
questions.explanation: Circumference \(C=2\pi r=2\pi \times \dfrac{44}{\pi}=88\) metres. The \(\pi\) cancels.

distractors.B: \(88\pi\) forgets to cancel the \(\pi\) in the radius.
distractors.C: \(196=14^{2}\) — area of a square, not relevant.
distractors.D: \(1\,936\pi=44^{2}\pi\) — area of a different circle, not the circumference.

questions.stem: A trapezium \(KLMN\) has parallel sides \(NM=12\) cm (top) and \(KL=20\) cm (bottom). If the area of the trapezium is \(80\text{ cm}^{2}\), then the height \(h\) is
questions.explanation: Area of a trapezium \(=\dfrac{1}{2}(a+b)h\). So \(80=\dfrac{1}{2}(12+20)h=16h\), giving \(h=\dfrac{80}{16}=5.0\) cm.

distractors.B: \(7.3\) might come from \(80/(12-1)\) or similar arithmetic slip.
distractors.C: \(8.0\) divides \(80\) by \(10\) — using only one of the parallel sides.
distractors.D: \(8.5\) divides \(80\) by \(\frac{12+20}{2}/2\) incorrectly.

diagram.alt: Trapezium KLMN with NM = 12 cm (top) and KL = 20 cm (bottom)

questions.stem: A compound solid shape consists of a triangular prism (with triangular height 3 cm) sitting on top of a rectangular-based prism. The width is 6 cm, the length is 10 cm and the rectangular prism has height 6 cm. The volume of the compound solid shape, in cm\(^{3}\), is
questions.explanation:
Triangular prism volume \(=\dfrac{1}{2}\times 6\times 3\times 10=90\text{ cm}^{3}\).

Rectangular prism volume \(=6\times 6\times 10=360\text{ cm}^{3}\).

Total \(=90+360=450\text{ cm}^{3}\).

distractors.A: \(90\) is only the triangular prism — forgets the rectangular base.
distractors.B: \(360\) is only the rectangular prism — forgets the triangular roof.
distractors.D: \(900\) treats the entire shape as a \(6\times 15\times 10\) box — overcounts.

diagram.alt: Compound solid: triangular prism on top of a rectangular-based prism

questions.stem: When a pilot left Country A to travel to Country B, the time on his watch was 22:50 hours. The distance between Country A and B was 3 000 km, which the pilot covered at an average speed of 625 km/h for the entire journey. Assuming both countries are in the same time zone, what was the time in Country B when the pilot arrived the next day?

distractors.C: \(03:50\) rounds the \(48\) min to a whole hour and adds incorrectly.
distractors.D: \(15:38\) is the AM/PM mistake (off by 12 hours).

questions.stem: Ms Ray recorded the 15 test scores of her students and, for reporting purposes, selected the score that appeared most often. Which of the following statistical measures did Ms Ray obtain?
questions.explanation: The mode is defined as the value that occurs most often in a data set. That is exactly what Ms Ray selected.

distractors.A: Mean is the arithmetic average of all values.
distractors.C: Range is the difference between the maximum and minimum.
distractors.D: Median is the middle value when data are arranged in order.

questions.stem: The cumulative frequency curve below shows the marks of 200 students who took a driving test. How many students scored AT MOST 45 marks?
questions.explanation: Read across from \(\text{mark}=45\) up to the curve, then across to the cumulative-frequency axis. The reading is approximately \(170\), so 170 students scored at most 45 marks.

distractors.A: \(34\) is roughly the cumulative frequency at a much lower mark (around 30), not at 45.
distractors.B: \(40\) is the cumulative frequency at a slightly higher mark than 30, not at 45.
distractors.C: \(166=200-34\) — that's how many scored MORE than the lower mark, not at most 45.

diagram.alt: Cumulative frequency curve of marks of 200 students

questions.stem: Using the same cumulative frequency curve as the previous item, the median mark scored by the 200 students is
questions.explanation: The median is at the \(\dfrac{200}{2}=100\)th student. Read across from cumulative frequency \(=100\) to the curve, then down to the mark axis. The mark is approximately \(37.5\).

distractors.C: \(48.5\) reads at the wrong cumulative frequency.
distractors.D: \(100.0\) is the cumulative frequency itself, not a mark.

diagram.alt: Cumulative frequency curve of marks of 200 students

questions.stem: A pie chart shows games played by 720 students. Tennis sector = \(36°\), Basketball = \(30\%\), Football has a right-angle sector. How many students play cricket OR football?
questions.explanation:
Football \(=90°\) (right-angle marker). Basketball \(=30\%\) of \(360°=108°\). Tennis \(=36°\). So Cricket \(=360-90-108-36=126°\).

Cricket OR Football \(=\dfrac{126+90}{360}\times 720=\dfrac{216}{360}\times 720=432\) students.

distractors.D: \(504=720-216\) — the OPPOSITE (those who play tennis or basketball).

diagram.alt: Pie chart of games played by 720 students (tennis 36°, basketball 30%, football 90°)

questions.stem: A student is picked at random from the same group of 720 students (Tennis \(=36°\), Football \(=90°\), Basketball \(=30\%\), Cricket the rest). What is the probability that the student plays NEITHER tennis NOR football?
questions.explanation: The 'neither tennis nor football' sectors are Basketball and Cricket. Their combined angle is \(360°-36°-90°=234°\). Probability \(=\dfrac{234}{360}=\dfrac{13}{20}\).

distractors.B: \(\frac{3}{10}\) is the probability of Basketball alone.

diagram.alt: Pie chart of games played by 720 students (tennis 36°, basketball 30%, football 90°)

questions.subtopic: Equation of a line from gradient and intercept
questions.stem: The equation of the line that crosses the vertical axis at the point \((0,5)\) and that has a gradient of 4 is
questions.explanation: The line has gradient \(m=4\) and \(y\)-intercept \(c=5\). Using \(y=mx+c\), the equation is \(y=4x+5\).

distractors.D: \(y=5x+4\) swaps gradient and \(y\)-intercept.

questions.stem: Which of the following graphs represents a linear function?
questions.explanation: A linear function has the form \(y=mx+c\) and graphs as a straight line. Option (A) shows a straight line — the only graph that fits.

distractors.B: A vertical line is NOT a function (it fails the vertical-line test).
distractors.C: A parabola is a quadratic function, not linear.
distractors.D: An exponential curve grows non-linearly.

questions.stem: Which of the following functions is undefined at the point \(x=-2\)?
questions.explanation: A rational function is undefined where its denominator equals 0. For \(\dfrac{5}{x+2}\), substituting \(x=-2\) gives a denominator of \(0\) — so the function is undefined there.

questions.subtopic: Identifying a function from a mapping
questions.stem: An arrow diagram shows a function \(f\) with mappings \(2\to 5\), \(5\to 8\), \(8\to 11\), \(11\to 14\). Which of the following equations BEST describes the function?
questions.explanation: Each output is exactly 3 more than the input: \(5=2+3\), \(8=5+3\), \(11=8+3\), \(14=11+3\). So \(y=x+3\).

distractors.B: \(x+y=3\) gives negative outputs for these inputs.
distractors.D: \(y=2(2)+1=5\) ✓ for the first pair but \(y=2(5)+1=11\neq 8\) for the second.

diagram.alt: Arrow diagram for function f mapping 2→5, 5→8, 8→11, 11→14

questions.stem: In the diagram, triangle \(PQR\) (with \(Q\) at the top, \(R\) lower-left, \(P\) lower-right) is mapped onto triangle \(P'Q'R'\) (with \(Q'\) at the bottom, \(R'\) upper-left, \(P'\) upper-right). What transformation maps \(PQR\) onto \(P'Q'R'\)?
questions.explanation: The image triangle is a 'flip' of the original — same size and shape but with vertices reversed across a horizontal mirror line. That is a reflection.

distractors.A: A rotation through \(180°\) would also flip but would also rotate the labels — here the labels read in the same circular order, indicating reflection.
distractors.C: A translation slides without flipping — the orientation here changes, so it is not a translation.
distractors.D: An enlargement changes the size — these triangles are the same size.

diagram.alt: Triangle PQR mapped onto P′Q′R′ — transformation type to identify

questions.stem: In which of the following polygons is the sum of the interior angles TWICE the sum of the exterior angles?
questions.explanation:
The sum of the exterior angles of any polygon is \(360°\). Twice that is \(720°\).

The sum of the interior angles of an \(n\)-sided polygon is \((n-2)\times 180°\). Setting \((n-2)\times 180=720\) gives \(n-2=4\), so \(n=6\) — a hexagon.

distractors.A: Triangle: interior \(=180°\), half of \(360°\) — too small.
distractors.D: Quadrilateral: interior \(=360°\), equal to (not double) the exterior sum.

questions.stem: Triangle \(ABC\) (with \(A\) at \((2,3)\), \(B\) at \((2,-2)\), \(C\) at \((1,-4)\)) is mapped onto triangle \(A'B'C'\) (with \(A'\) at \((-3,6)\), \(B'\) at \((-3,1)\), \(C'\) at \((-4,-1)\)). The translation by which \(ABC\) is mapped onto \(A'B'C'\) is represented by
questions.explanation: Compare any vertex with its image: \(A(2,3)\to A'(-3,6)\). The change is \((-3-2,\ 6-3)=(-5,\ 3)\). So the translation vector is \(\begin{pmatrix}-5\\ 3\end{pmatrix}\).

distractors.D: \(\begin{pmatrix}5\\ -3\end{pmatrix}\) reverses both signs.

diagram.alt: Triangle ABC mapped onto A″B″C″ via composite transformation on coordinate plane

questions.stem: The point \(P(2,-3)\) is rotated about the origin through an angle of \(90°\) in an anti-clockwise direction. What are the coordinates of the image \(P'\)?
questions.explanation: A \(90°\) anti-clockwise rotation about the origin sends \((x,y)\to(-y,x)\). So \((2,-3)\to(3,\ 2)\).

distractors.B: (2, 3) just makes the y-coordinate positive — that's a reflection in the x-axis.
distractors.C: (-3, 2) is the result of a \(90°\) clockwise rotation, not anti-clockwise.
distractors.D: (3, -2) is a \(180°\) rotation.

questions.stem: In a right-angled triangle \(ABC\) with the right angle at \(B\), the side \(BC=4\) cm (vertical), the hypotenuse \(AC=8\) cm, and the angle at \(A\) is labelled \(x\) (and at \(C\) is labelled \(y\)). Which trigonometric ratio is equal to \(\dfrac{4}{8}\)?
questions.explanation: \(BC\) is opposite to angle \(x\) (at \(A\)). \(AC\) is the hypotenuse. So \(\sin x=\dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}=\dfrac{BC}{AC}=\dfrac{4}{8}\).

distractors.C: \(\tan y=\dfrac{AB}{4}\) — wrong sides for the ratio \(\tfrac{4}{8}\).

diagram.alt: Right-angled triangle ABC with right angle at B, BC = 4 cm, AC = 8 cm

questions.stem: Lines \(OAA'\), \(OBB'\) and \(OCC'\) are straight. Triangle \(ABC\) is mapped onto triangle \(A'B'C'\) by an enlargement with centre \(O\). On the diagram, side \(BC=2\) units and the corresponding side \(B'C'=4\) units. What is the scale factor of the enlargement?
questions.explanation: Scale factor \(=\dfrac{\text{image length}}{\text{object length}}=\dfrac{B'C'}{BC}=\dfrac{4}{2}=2\). Each side of the image triangle is twice the corresponding side of the original.

distractors.A: \(\frac{1}{4}\) inverts the ratio and uses the wrong values.
distractors.B: \(\frac{1}{2}\) inverts the correct ratio (object/image instead of image/object).
distractors.D: \(4\) is the image length, not the ratio.

diagram.alt: Triangles ABC and A′B′C′ — enlargement with centre O

questions.stem: What percentage of 40 is 8?
questions.explanation:
To find what percentage one number is of another, divide and multiply by 100.

\[\frac{8}{40}\times 100\% = \frac{1}{5}\times 100\% = 20\%\]

distractors.A: Computed \(40 \div 8 = 5\) and forgot the percentage step.
distractors.C: Subtracted \(40 - 8 = 32\) instead of taking a ratio.
distractors.D: Treated 8 as the whole and computed \(\tfrac{40}{8}\times 100 \div ?\) loosely.

questions.stem: \(\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^{-2}\) is equal to
questions.explanation:
A negative index means take the reciprocal, then apply the positive power.

\[\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{-2} = \left(\tfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2} = \tfrac{9}{4}\]

distractors.A: Squared \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) and ignored the negative index.
distractors.B: Doubled top and bottom instead of squaring.
distractors.C: Took the reciprocal but forgot to square.

questions.explanation:
Square first, then subtract, then take the square root.

\[\sqrt{17^{2}-15^{2}} = \sqrt{289-225} = \sqrt{64} = 8\]

distractors.D: Squared the difference: \((17-15)^2\).

questions.stem: The square root of 191 lies between
questions.explanation:
Identify nearby perfect squares.

\(13^{2}=169\) and \(14^{2}=196\), and \(169<191<196\), so \(\sqrt{191}\) is between 13 and 14.

distractors.D: Halved 191 instead of square-rooting.

questions.stem: \(99 \times 101\) has the same value as
questions.explanation: Use the distributive law: \(99\times 101 = 99(100+1) = (99\times 100) + (99\times 1)\).

distractors.B: Multiplied the two parts instead of adding.

questions.stem: There are 40 students in a class. Girls make up 60% of the class. 25% of the girls wear glasses. How many girls in the class wear glasses?
questions.explanation:
First find the number of girls, then take 25% of that.

\[\text{Girls} = 60\%\times 40 = 24\]

\[\text{Wear glasses} = 25\%\times 24 = 6\]

distractors.D: Took \(60\%\) of 25, mixing the two percentages.

questions.stem: If \(n(P)=x\), then the number of subsets of \(P\) can be expressed as
questions.explanation: For a set with \(n\) elements, the number of subsets is \(2^{n}\). Here \(n(P)=x\), so the count is \(2^{x}\).

distractors.B: Squared the count instead of using a power of 2.
distractors.C: Doubled the count instead of using \(2^{x}\).
distractors.D: Combined two formulas incorrectly.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements describes the set of integers from \(-3\) but less than \(6\)?
questions.explanation:
"From \(-3\) but less than 6" excludes both endpoints, so use strict inequalities.

\[-3 < x < 6,\quad x \in \mathbb{Z}\]

questions.stem: Which of the following pairs of sets is an example of disjoint sets?
questions.explanation: Disjoint sets share no elements. Vowels and consonants partition the alphabet, so they have no letters in common.

distractors.B: 6, 12, 18 are multiples of both 2 and 3, so they overlap.
distractors.C: Many primes (3, 5, 7) are odd, so they overlap.
distractors.D: All Caribbean islands lie in the tropics, so they overlap.

questions.stem: Given \(P=\{1,3,6,8,9,12,15\}\) and \(Q=\{6,9,12\}\), which of the following statements is true?
questions.explanation: Every element of \(Q\) (\(6, 9, 12\)) is in \(P\), so \(Q\) is a proper subset of \(P\): \(Q \subset P\).

distractors.C: They share elements, so they are not disjoint.
distractors.D: Complement requires a universal set; \(P\) contains \(Q\).

questions.stem: The 30 students in Teacher May's class have either a dog (D) or a cat (C), or none of the two. ALL the students who have a cat also have a dog. If \(n(D)=12\) and \(n(C)=16\), which Venn diagram correctly represents this information?
questions.explanation: "All cat owners also own a dog" means \(C \subset D\). The correct diagram shows the cat circle nested inside the dog circle, with the rest of the class outside both circles.

distractors.A: Treats \(D\) and \(C\) as overlapping but not nested.
distractors.B: Shows them as disjoint, contradicting the cat-implies-dog rule.
distractors.C: Reverses the inclusion: dog inside cat.

questions.stem: In which of the following Venn diagrams is the region \(P \cap Q'\) shaded?
questions.explanation: \(P \cap Q'\) means "in \(P\) and not in \(Q\)" — the part of circle \(P\) that lies outside circle \(Q\).

distractors.D: That is \((P \cup Q)'\).

questions.stem: If EC$2.50 is equivalent to US$1.00, then EC$20.00 in US dollars is
questions.explanation:
Divide the EC amount by the rate.

\[\text{US\$} = \frac{20.00}{2.50} = 8.00\]

So EC$20.00 = US$8.00.

distractors.D: Multiplied by 4 instead of dividing by 2.5.

questions.stem: The simple interest on $600 for \(t\) years at 5% per annum is $120. The value of \(t\) is
questions.explanation:
From \(I = \dfrac{P\,R\,T}{100}\), rearrange for \(T\):

\[T = \frac{100\,I}{P\,R} = \frac{100\times 120}{600\times 5}\]

distractors.D: Forgot to divide \(P\) and \(R\) into the formula.

questions.stem: Given that \(y\) varies directly as \(x\), and \(y=19\) when \(x=152\), which equation represents the relationship between \(y\) and \(x\)?
questions.explanation:
Direct variation gives \(y = kx\). Substitute the values to find \(k\).

\[19 = k(152) \Rightarrow k = \tfrac{19}{152} = \tfrac{1}{8}\]

So \(y = \tfrac{1}{8}x\).

distractors.D: Treated direct variation as a difference.

questions.stem: A man's annual salary is $45 000. His tax-free allowances total $13 000. He has to pay tax of 20% on his taxable income. The tax payable is
questions.explanation:
Subtract allowances first to get the taxable income, then take 20%.

\[\text{Taxable} = 45\,000 - 13\,000 = 32\,000\]

\[\text{Tax} = 20\%\times 32\,000 = \$6\,400\]

distractors.A: Took 20% of the allowance ($13 000) instead of the taxable income.
distractors.D: Subtracted tax from salary instead of computing tax.

questions.stem: A man pays 60 cents for every 200 m\(^{3}\) of gas used, plus a fixed charge of $13.75. How much does he pay when he uses 55 000 m\(^{3}\) of gas?
questions.explanation:
Find the number of 200 m\(^{3}\) blocks, multiply by $0.60, then add the fixed charge.

\[\frac{55\,000}{200} = 275 \text{ blocks}\]

\[\text{Variable} = 275 \times 0.60 = \$165.00\]

\[\text{Total} = 165.00 + 13.75 = \$178.75\]

distractors.B: Subtracted the fixed charge instead of adding.
distractors.C: Forgot to add the $13.75 fixed charge.
distractors.D: Used 250 blocks (rounded \(\tfrac{55\,000}{220}\) wrongly).

questions.stem: A car presently valued at $12 000 depreciates at the rate of 10% per annum. What will be the value of the car one year later?
questions.explanation:
Depreciation reduces the value: subtract 10% of $12 000.

\[10\%\times 12\,000 = \$1\,200\]

\[\text{New value} = 12\,000 - 1\,200 = \$10\,800\]

distractors.D: Added 10% instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: A dinner at a restaurant was advertised at $60 plus 18% tax. The TOTAL bill for this dinner was
questions.explanation:
Compute 18% of $60 and add it to the bill.

\[\text{Tax} = 0.18 \times 60 = \$10.80\]

\[\text{Total} = 60 + 10.80 = \$70.80\]

distractors.D: Added 36% (doubled the tax).

questions.stem: A store offers a discount of 10% to customers who spend more than $20. If a customer's total bill is $80, what will he actually pay?
questions.explanation:
Take 10% off the $80 bill.

\[\text{Discount} = 0.10 \times 80 = \$8\]

\[\text{Pay} = 80 - 8 = \$72\]

distractors.D: Subtracted $6 (took 7.5% by mistake).

questions.stem: \(\dfrac{4}{x} + \dfrac{2}{x} =\)
questions.explanation:
Same denominator, so add the numerators.

\[\frac{4}{x}+\frac{2}{x} = \frac{4+2}{x} = \frac{6}{x}\]

distractors.D: Doubled the denominator while adding numerators.

questions.explanation:
Substitute \(p=5\) and \(q=2\) into the binary operation.

\[5 * 2 = \sqrt{5^{3} - 2^{2}} = \sqrt{125 - 4} = \sqrt{121} = 11\]

questions.stem: Given that \(3(x-1) - 2(x-1) = 7\), the value of \(x\) is
questions.explanation:
Expand and collect like terms.

\[3x-3-2x+2 = 7\]

\[x-1 = 7 \Rightarrow x = 8\]

distractors.B: Used the right-hand side directly without isolating \(x\).
distractors.D: Added an extra 1 instead of subtracting.

questions.explanation:
Two negatives become a positive, then collect like terms.

\[-(-2x) - 3x = 2x - 3x = -x\]

distractors.D: Added: \(2x+3x=5x\).

questions.stem: If \(x=4\) and \(y=2\), then the value of \(\dfrac{x^{2}+3y}{xy}\) is
questions.explanation:
Substitute and simplify.

\[\frac{x^{2}+3y}{xy} = \frac{16+6}{8} = \frac{22}{8} = \frac{11}{4} = 2\tfrac{3}{4}\]

questions.stem: If \(\dfrac{x}{5} = 20\), then \(x =\)
questions.explanation:
Multiply both sides by 5 to undo the division.

\[\frac{x}{5} = 20 \Rightarrow x = 20 \times 5\]

distractors.A: Subtracted instead of using inverse operation.
distractors.C: Divided again, repeating the operation already applied.

questions.stem: In a parallelogram \(ABCD\), \(AB\) is parallel to \(DC\) and \(AD\) is parallel to \(BC\). Given \(\vec{AB}=\mathbf{x}\) and \(\vec{AD}=\mathbf{y}\), then \(\vec{AC}\) expressed in terms of \(\mathbf{x}\) and \(\mathbf{y}\) is
questions.explanation:
By the triangle law, \(\vec{AC} = \vec{AB} + \vec{BC}\). Since \(\vec{BC} = \vec{AD} = \mathbf{y}\),

\[\vec{AC} = \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\]

distractors.D: Negated both vectors.

diagram.alt: In a parallelogram ABCD, AB is parallel to DC and AD is parallel to BC

questions.stem: If \(5\begin{pmatrix}a\\b\end{pmatrix} = 4\begin{pmatrix}10\\20\end{pmatrix}\), then the values of \(a\) and \(b\) are
questions.explanation:
Multiply out the right side, then equate corresponding entries.

\[\begin{pmatrix}5a\\5b\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}40\\80\end{pmatrix}\]

\[a = \tfrac{40}{5} = 8,\quad b = \tfrac{80}{5} = 16\]

distractors.C: Inverted: divided 10 by 4 instead of multiplying.
distractors.D: Ignored the scalars.

questions.stem: Given \(M=\begin{pmatrix}1 & 3 & -3\\3 & 0 & 5\end{pmatrix}\) and \(N=\begin{pmatrix}3 & 0\\2 & 1\\0 & 5\end{pmatrix}\), the matrix product \(MN\) is

distractors.D: Transposed the answer.

questions.stem: The volume of a cube whose edge is 6 cm long is
questions.explanation:
Volume of a cube is \(s^{3}\) where \(s\) is the edge length.

\[V = 6^{3} = 216\text{ cm}^{3}\]

questions.stem: If it took a speedboat 9 hours to travel a distance of 1 080 km, what was its average speed, in km h\(^{-1}\)?
questions.explanation:
Average speed is distance divided by time.

\[\text{Speed} = \frac{1080}{9} = 120 \text{ km h}^{-1}\]

distractors.D: Multiplied instead of dividing.

questions.stem: 2 500 millimetres expressed in metres is
questions.explanation:
1 metre = 1 000 millimetres, so divide by 1 000.

\[2500 \div 1000 = 2.5 \text{ m}\]

distractors.D: Divided by 10.

questions.stem: An aircraft leaves \(P\) at 16:00 hours and arrives at \(Q\) at 19:30 hours, travelling at an average speed of 550 kilometres per hour. \(P\) and \(Q\) are in the same time zone. The distance from \(P\) to \(Q\), in kilometres, is
questions.explanation:
Find the time elapsed, then \(\text{distance} = \text{speed} \times \text{time}\).

\[\text{Time} = 19{:}30 - 16{:}00 = 3.5 \text{ h}\]

\[\text{Distance} = 550 \times 3.5 = 1925 \text{ km}\]

questions.stem: In a rectangular garden plot, 15 m long and 12 m wide, an area of 80 m\(^{2}\) is used for a vegetable garden. What area of the plot is NOT used for vegetable gardening?
questions.explanation:
Find the total area, then subtract the vegetable area.

\[\text{Total} = 15 \times 12 = 180 \text{ m}^{2}\]

\[\text{Not used} = 180 - 80 = 100 \text{ m}^{2}\]

distractors.D: Used 340 m\(^{2}\) total.

questions.stem: The distance around a lake is 8 km. On a map, this distance around the lake is represented by a length of 2 cm. The scale on the map is
questions.explanation:
Convert 8 km to cm: \(8 \text{ km} = 800\,000 \text{ cm}\). Then form a ratio and simplify.

\[2 : 800\,000 = 1 : 400\,000\]

distractors.C: Forgot to divide by 2 at the end.

questions.stem: The volume of a cuboid is 320 cm\(^{3}\) and the height is 5 cm. If the cuboid has a square base, what is the length of one side of the base?
questions.explanation:
For a square base of side \(x\), \(V = x^{2}h\).

\[320 = 5x^{2} \Rightarrow x^{2} = 64 \Rightarrow x = 8 \text{ cm}\]

distractors.D: Took \(64\) without square-rooting.

questions.stem: A square has the same area as a rectangle with sides of length 9 cm and 16 cm. What is the length of the side of the square?
questions.explanation: The rectangle area is \(9 \times 16 = 144\) cm\(^{2}\). For the square, side \(=\sqrt{144}=12\) cm.

distractors.A: Used the shorter side of the rectangle.
distractors.D: Halved the area instead of square-rooting.

questions.stem: The median of the numbers 1, 1, 5, 5, 6, 7, 7, 7, 7, 8 is
questions.explanation:
There are 10 values, so the median is the average of the 5th and 6th values.

\[\text{Median} = \frac{6+7}{2} = 6.5\]

distractors.D: Took the mode.

questions.stem: A frequency table groups times in minutes as 0-2, 3-5, 6-8, 9-11. The lower class boundary of the interval "3 - 5" is
questions.explanation:
The lower class boundary is halfway between the upper limit of the previous class and the lower limit of this class.

\[\text{LCB} = \frac{2+3}{2} = 2.5\]

distractors.A: Took the upper limit of the previous class.
distractors.C: Took the lower class limit (not boundary).
distractors.D: Took the upper boundary.

questions.stem: A frequency table gives the scores 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 11 with frequencies 8, 4, 6, 3, 12, 2 respectively. The modal score is
questions.explanation: The mode is the score with the greatest frequency. The score 8 has the highest frequency (12 students), so the modal score is 8.

distractors.B: Took the mean of frequencies near the top.
distractors.C: Took the midpoint of the top two scores.
distractors.D: Took the highest frequency value (12) instead of the score.

questions.stem: A cumulative frequency curve shows the marks obtained by 200 students on a test. Using the curve at cumulative frequency 100, the median of the marks scored is
questions.explanation: The median from a cumulative frequency curve is found by reading off the mark at \(\tfrac{N}{2} = 100\). From the graph, this gives 37.5.

distractors.D: Confused cumulative frequency value with mark.

diagram.alt: A cumulative frequency curve shows the marks obtained by 200 students on a test

questions.stem: A bar chart shows the ages of children who took part in a survey, with bars of heights 3, 4, 2, 5 and 1. How many children took part in the survey?
questions.explanation:
Sum the heights of the bars to get the total number of children.

\[3+4+2+5+1 = 15\]

distractors.C: Multiplied the bar heights instead of adding.
distractors.D: Misread the scale.

diagram.alt: A bar chart shows the ages of children who took part in a survey, with bars of heights 3,

questions.stem: Six hundred students write an examination. The probability of a randomly selected student failing the examination is \(\tfrac{1}{5}\). How many students are expected to pass?
questions.explanation:
\(P(\text{pass}) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{4}{5}\). Then multiply by the total.

\[\text{Expected pass} = \tfrac{4}{5}\times 600 = 480\]

distractors.D: Subtracted 100 from 600.

questions.stem: A mapping diagram shows two arrows from different elements of the domain landing on the same element of the co-domain. The relationship that BEST describes the mapping is
questions.explanation: Several inputs map to the same output, so it is a many-to-one mapping.

distractors.A: True only when each input has a distinct output.
distractors.B: Would require one input mapping to multiple outputs.
distractors.D: Both sides would have multiple connections.

diagram.alt: A mapping diagram shows two arrows from different elements of the domain landing on the

questions.stem: An arrow diagram links 2 to 8 and 10, 3 to 9, and 4 to 8. The arrow diagram BEST describes the relation
questions.explanation: 2 is a factor of 8 and 10; 3 is a factor of 9; 4 is a factor of 8. So the relation is "is a factor of".

distractors.A: True for some pairs (e.g. \(2<8\)) but doesn't capture the divisibility rule for all arrows.
distractors.B: Reversed inequality; doesn't fit since \(2<8\), not \(2>8\).
distractors.C: Reverses the correct relation: 8 is a multiple of 2, not the other way round.

diagram.alt: An arrow diagram links 2 to 8 and 10, 3 to 9, and 4 to 8

questions.stem: Which of the following line graphs represents \(\{x : -2 < x \leq 4\}\)?
questions.explanation: \(x>-2\) is strict (open circle at \(-2\)) and \(x \leq 4\) is inclusive (filled circle at 4).

distractors.A: Reverses the inclusion at the endpoints.
distractors.B: Both endpoints inclusive (would be \(\leq\) at both).
distractors.C: Both endpoints excluded (would be strict at both).

questions.stem: The maximum point of \(y = 4x - x^{2}\) is
questions.explanation: Complete the square: \(y = -(x-2)^{2}+4\). The maximum is at \((2, 4)\).

distractors.D: Swapped \(x\) and \(y\) of the vertex.

diagram.alt: The maximum point of y = 4x - x^{2} is

questions.stem: Which of the following sets is represented by the function \(f : x \mapsto x^{2}+3\) where \(x \in \{0,1,2,3\}\)?
A line \(L\) is parallel to the line \(2x - 5y - 8 = 0\). What is the gradient of the line \(L\)?
questions.explanation:
Rearrange to \(y = mx + c\) form.

\[2x - 5y - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow y = \tfrac{2}{5}x - \tfrac{8}{5}\]

So gradient \(= \tfrac{2}{5}\). Parallel lines share the same gradient.

distractors.D: Used the coefficient 2 of \(x\) directly.

questions.stem: A point \(B(2,5)\) is mapped onto \(B'(4,6)\) by a translation. The translation vector is
questions.explanation:
Subtract image and object positions component-wise.

\[\binom{4}{6} - \binom{2}{5} = \binom{2}{1}\]

distractors.D: Added the original components.

diagram.alt: A point B(2,5) is mapped onto B'(4,6) by a translation

questions.stem: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. The angles \(x\) and \(y\) lie on opposite sides of the transversal and between the two parallel lines. Which of the following BEST describes the relation between \(x\) and \(y\)?
questions.explanation: The angles described are alternate angles. Alternate angles between parallel lines are equal, so \(x = y\).

distractors.B: There is no inequality relation for parallel-line angles.
distractors.C: Differences of 180° are not a standard angle relationship.
distractors.D: Sum of 180° applies to co-interior (allied) angles, not alternate ones.

diagram.alt: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal

questions.stem: A ship sailed 8 km due east from \(P\) to \(Q\). It then sailed 6 km due north to \(R\). Which of the following diagrams BEST represents the path of the ship?
questions.explanation: Going east is to the right; going north is upward. The correct diagram shows an 8 km eastward leg followed by a 6 km northward leg, forming an L-shape.

distractors.A: Second leg goes south instead of north.
distractors.B: First leg goes west instead of east.

distractors.D: Multiplied by \(1000\) — converted to mm by mistake.

questions.stem: A circle, centre \(O\), is divided into a minor sector of central angle \(60^\circ\) and a major sector. If the area of the major sector is \(515\,\text{cm}^{2}\), then the area of the circle, in \(\text{cm}^{2}\), is
questions.explanation: Major sector angle \(=360^\circ-60^\circ=300^\circ\). Since \(300^\circ\) corresponds to area \(515\), \(60^\circ\) corresponds to \(\dfrac{515}{5}=103\). The full circle (\(360^\circ\)) has area \(515+103=618\,\text{cm}^{2}\).

distractors.A: This is the minor sector area, not the whole circle.
distractors.B: Computed \(515-103=412\) — that is not the whole circle area.

questions.stem: The volume of a cuboid with a square base is \(320\,\text{cm}^{3}\). If the side of the square base is \(8\,\text{cm}\), what is the value of the height \(h\), in cm?
questions.explanation: Volume \(=l\times w\times h\). So \(320=8\times 8\times h=64h\), giving \(h=\dfrac{320}{64}=5\,\text{cm}\).

distractors.C: Used \(8\times h=320\) — divided by only one side.
distractors.D: Mistook \(64\) (the base area) for the height.

questions.stem: The area of a triangle is \(60\,\text{cm}^{2}\) and its height is \(10\,\text{cm}\). What is the length of the base of the triangle, in cm?

distractors.A: Forgot the factor of \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) on the wrong side: \(60\div 10=6\).
distractors.D: Used \(b+h=60-10\) reasoning — invented identity.

questions.explanation: Total \(=29+47+24+39+11=150\). Median position \(=\dfrac{150+1}{2}=75.5\)th. Cumulative frequencies: \(29\), \(76\), ... — the 75.5th value falls in the class \(31\!-\!40\). Lower class limit \(=31\).

distractors.B: Picked the next class up — off-by-one in cumulative count.
distractors.C: Picked the middle class by position rather than cumulative frequency.
distractors.D: Picked the highest-mass class incorrectly.

questions.stem: Using the same distribution, how many children have a mass of at MOST \(50\,\text{kg}\)?
questions.explanation: Sum the frequencies of classes up to and including \(41\!-\!50\): \(29+47+24=100\).

distractors.B: Used the upper class limit \(50\) as the count.

questions.subtopic: Lower quartile from cumulative frequency curve
questions.stem: The cumulative frequency curve shows the marks obtained by \(200\) students on a test. The lower quartile of the marks is
questions.explanation: Lower quartile is at the \(\dfrac{n+1}{4}=\dfrac{201}{4}\approx 50.25\)th value. Reading across the curve at cumulative frequency \(50\) gives a mark of \(30.0\).

distractors.B: Read at cumulative frequency \(75\) (incorrect quartile position).
distractors.C: Took the median position (\(100\)) and read off, not the lower quartile.
distractors.D: Read the upper quartile position by mistake.
