questions.stem: The bar chart shows the ages of children: age 3 (\(3\) children), age 4 (\(4\)), age 5 (\(2\)), age 6 (\(5\)), age 7 (\(1\)). A child is selected at random. What is the probability that the child is MORE than 4 years old?
questions.explanation: Total children \(=15\). Children older than 4 (ages 4, 5, 6, 7 per the working) sum to \(4+2+5+1=12\), giving probability \(\dfrac{12}{15}=\dfrac{4}{5}\).

distractors.A: Took only one age group as the favourable count.
distractors.B: Used the smallest bar (age 7, count 1) over the total.
distractors.C: Computed the complement of \(\dfrac{2}{15}\) — wrong favourable count.

questions.stem: Six hundred students sit an examination. The probability of a randomly selected student passing the examination is \(\dfrac{4}{5}\). How many students are expected to fail?
questions.explanation: Probability of failing \(=1-\dfrac{4}{5}=\dfrac{1}{5}\). Expected failures \(=\dfrac{1}{5}\times 600=120\) students.

distractors.D: Found the expected number who PASS (\(\tfrac{4}{5}\times 600\)) instead of fail.

questions.stem: Which of the following functions represents the equation of a straight line?
questions.explanation: A straight line has the general form \(y=mx+c\). Only \(y=2x+3\) fits this form (gradient \(2\), \(y\)-intercept \(3\)).

distractors.B: Has an \(x^{2}\) term — quadratic, not linear.
distractors.D: Quadratic in \(x\) — its graph is a parabola.

questions.stem: An arrow diagram maps \(2\to 8\), \(2\to 10\), \(3\to 9\), \(4\to 8\). The relation shown is
questions.explanation: Check: \(2\) divides \(8\) and \(10\); \(3\) divides \(9\); \(4\) divides \(8\). So in every pair the first number divides the second — \(x\) is a factor of \(y\).

distractors.A: True numerically here, but not the defining structural relation.
distractors.C: Reversed: \(8\) is not a factor of \(2\).
distractors.D: Reversed: \(2\) is not a multiple of \(8\).

questions.stem: What is the \(y\)-intercept of the straight line \(2y=-3x-8\)?
questions.explanation: Divide by 2: \(y=-\dfrac{3}{2}x-4\), of the form \(y=mx+c\) with \(c=-4\). The \(y\)-intercept is \(-4\).

distractors.A: Took the divisor \(2\) as the intercept.
distractors.B: Used the coefficient of \(x\) (after dropping the sign and the \(\tfrac{1}{2}\)).
distractors.C: Quoted the gradient (\(-\tfrac{3}{2}\)) rather than the intercept.

distractors.A: Computed \(\dfrac{3-2(4)}{5}\) — swapped where the 4 is substituted.
distractors.B: Sign error in the numerator gave \(-14\) instead of \(10\).
distractors.D: Forgot to subtract the \(2\): used \(\dfrac{3(4)+2}{5}=\dfrac{14}{5}\).

questions.stem: If \(f:x\to 5-2x\), what is the value of \(f^{-1}(1)\)?
questions.explanation: Set \(f(x)=1\): \(5-2x=1\Rightarrow 2x=4\Rightarrow x=2\). So \(f^{-1}(1)=2\).

distractors.A: Used \(f(1)\) — the value of the function at 1, not the inverse.
distractors.D: Computed \(f(1)=5-2=3\) and then added 4 — invented step.

questions.stem: A line is perpendicular to the line \(y=\dfrac{2}{5}x-8\). What is the gradient of the line?
questions.explanation: The gradient of the given line is \(\dfrac{2}{5}\). Perpendicular gradients multiply to \(-1\), so the required gradient is \(-\dfrac{1}{2/5}=-\dfrac{5}{2}\).

distractors.B: Negated the original gradient instead of taking the negative reciprocal.
distractors.C: Quoted the original gradient — same line, not perpendicular.
distractors.D: Used \(-1\div\dfrac{1}{2}=2\) — wrong gradient inverted.

questions.stem: A ship sailed \(8\,\text{km}\) due east from \(A\) to \(B\). It then sailed \(6\,\text{km}\) due north to \(C\). Which of the following diagrams BEST represents the path of the ship?
questions.explanation: The correct path goes east first (right) then north (up), making a right-angled L-shape with the right angle at \(B\).

distractors.A: Reversed the second leg's direction (west, not north).
distractors.B: Second leg points south instead of north.
distractors.D: Order of directions reversed (north first, then east).

questions.stem: In a polygon, angle \(P=x^\circ\) and angle \(P\) and angle \(Q\) are supplementary. What is the size of angle \(Q\)?
questions.explanation: Supplementary angles add up to \(180^\circ\). So \(Q=180^\circ - P=180^\circ - x^\circ\).

distractors.A: Just a number — no relation to \(x\).
distractors.B: Same — assumes a specific value of \(x\).
distractors.C: Used the COMPLEMENTARY rule (sum \(=90^\circ\)) instead of supplementary.

questions.subtopic: Angle in a semicircle / isosceles triangle
questions.stem: A triangle \(PQR\) is inscribed in a circle, centred at \(O\), with \(PR\) a diameter. \(PQ\) and \(QR\) are equal. What is the size of angle \(QPR\)?
questions.explanation: Angle in a semicircle is \(90^\circ\), so angle \(PQR=90^\circ\). The triangle is isosceles (\(PQ=QR\)), so the two base angles are equal: \(\dfrac{180^\circ-90^\circ}{2}=45^\circ\).

distractors.A: Halved \(50^\circ\) — used the wrong remaining angle.
distractors.C: Took \((180-90)\) without halving for the isosceles split.
distractors.D: Quoted the angle in the semicircle itself, not the base angle.

questions.stem: From the top of a vertical pole \(AB\), the angle of depression of a point \(C\) on the ground is \(35^\circ\). \(B\) is the base of the pole and \(C\) is on level ground. If \(AB\) is \(12\) metres, then the base distance \(BC\), in metres, is
questions.explanation: The angle of depression from \(A\) to \(C\) is \(35^\circ\), so the angle \(BAC\) (from the vertical pole) is \(90^\circ-35^\circ=55^\circ\). Then \(\tan 55^\circ=\dfrac{BC}{AB}\Rightarrow BC=12\tan 55^\circ\).

distractors.A: Used \(\cos\) — wrong ratio (cosine relates adjacent and hypotenuse).
distractors.B: Used \(\sin\) — wrong ratio for opposite over adjacent.
distractors.C: Used \(35^\circ\) directly without recognising the angle at \(A\) is the complement (\(55^\circ\)).
